title: Fragmentation Temperature of 1D and 3D Quantum Droplets in a BEC Mixture
authors: Jeroen Van Loock, Denise Ahmed-Braun, Jacques Tempere
abstract: In a mixture of two Bose-Einstein condensates, the interactions can be tuned such that self-bound objects called quantum droplets appear. Whereas the ground states of such quantum droplets at finite temperature have been studied for three- and one-dimensional configurations, the possible fragmentation of these droplets has so far not been considered in these studies. In this paper, we show that droplets can lower their free energy by splitting or fragmenting in a combination of multiple smaller droplets and/or a gas. Three-dimensional droplets will split when the interspecies interaction strength is considerably stronger than the intraspecies interaction strength, and the number of atoms is of the same order as the minimum number of atoms necessary to form a droplet. One-dimensional droplets will fragment as long as the intraspecies and interspecies interactions strength do not vary too much in strength and the density is not to big compared with the scattering length. If the temperature rises, 1D droplets will split by expelling atoms, forming a gas of predominantly free atoms and pairs of atoms. These pairs remain present in the system up to considerably high temperatures compared to the transition temperature. Our results provide important insights on the stability of these droplets.
summary: This paper explores how quantum droplets in BEC mixtures fragment into multiple pieces or a gas as temperature increases. It provides a theoretical description for both 1D and 3D systems, showing that fragmentation is driven by entropy. In 3D, fragmentation is linked to low atom numbers and strong interspecies interactions, while in 1D, rising temperatures cause droplets to expel atoms into a gas of free atoms and pairs. The results offer critical insights into the stability and lifetime of these self-bound states in ultracold gases.
main_problem: The study investigates the fragmentation transition of 1D and 3D quantum droplets in Bose-Einstein condensate mixtures at finite temperatures, driven by the competition between energy and entropy.
main_result: The authors identify a fragmentation temperature (Tf) where droplets split into multiple smaller droplets or a gas; in 3D, this occurs at low atom numbers and high interspecies interaction, while in 1D, droplets expel atoms to form a gas of free atoms and pairs as temperature rises.
method: The study uses the extended Gross-Pitaevskii equation (eGPE) with Lee-Huang-Yang corrections, a thermodynamic framework involving partition functions, and numerical imaginary time propagation.
model_or_system: A mixture of two Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs) in 1D and 3D configurations, modeled with a Hamiltonian including kinetic energy, external potentials, and s-wave contact interactions (intraspecies and interspecies).
key_observables: Fragmentation temperature (Tf), average fragmentation rate (number of fragments), specific heat (Cv), and droplet density profiles.
important_parameters: Interspecies interaction strength (g12), intraspecies interaction strength (gjj), atom number (N), temperature (T), system size (L or V), and the interaction deviation (delta g).
main_assumptions: The system is in the ultracold regime; droplets are treated as non-interacting; the 3D model requires regularization/renormalization; the 1D model assumes the droplet expels atoms into a gas or small clusters.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows ground state density profiles and energy vs. atom number; Figure 6 displays the average fragmentation rate and specific heat vs. temperature for 1D systems; Figure 7 shows the fragmentation temperature as a function of interaction strength and density.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of droplet fragmentation, establishes the 1D and 3D models using eGPE and LHY corrections, develops a thermodynamic framework for fragmentation, presents results for 3D stability and fragmentation, details 1D fragmentation and temperature effects, and concludes with scaling analysis.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and AMO physics as it explores the thermodynamic stability and phase transitions of self-bound quantum objects, providing new insights into how thermal fluctuations can destroy quantum droplets.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 In Fig. a, the ground state order parameter for a three-dimensional quantum droplet as a function of the radial distance ˜r is plotted for diﬀerent atom numbers ˜ N. In Fig. b, the ground state energy for a three-dimensional quantum droplet ( ˜E) as a function of the number of atoms is plotted, where ˜ Nc is the critical particle number.

Fig. 2 Fig. a shows the ground state order parameter for a one-dimensional quantum droplet as a function of the distance ˜r for diﬀerent atom numbers ˜ N. Fig. b shows the ground state energy for a one-dimensional quantum droplet ( ˜E) as a function of the number of atoms.

Fig. 3 The average fragmentation rate (⟨v⟩) of 3D droplets for the equal part conﬁguration that is derived in Eq. (55), is given as a function of the temperature for diﬀerent number of particles for |δg|/g = 0.4 and V = 1012a3. The vertical lines corresponds to the fragmentation temperature, calculated with Eq. (57). TC is the critical temperature [21] and divides the green zone where droplets can exist, with the blue zone where they can not exist in the limit of V →∞.

Fig. 4 In Fig. a, the fragmentation temperature (Tf ) is plotted for 3D droplets that are just able to split into smaller droplets that are slightly larger than Nc ( ˜ N/N max n = 18.7 ≈18.65 = ˜ Nc). In Fig. b, the fragmentation temperature (Tf ) is plotted for droplets that are just not able to split into smaller droplets with Nc atoms ( ˜ N/(N max n + 1) = 18.6 ≈˜ Nc). In both ﬁgures, Tf is given for diﬀerent droplet sizes. These droplets will thus split in diﬀerent numbers of fragments (N max n ). In both ﬁgures Tf is plotted as a function of |δg|/g for a system with a volume V = 1012a3. In Fig. c, the corresponding number of atoms is given for Fig. a. There is no visual diﬀerence with...

Fig. 5 In this ﬁgure the ground state of a 1D droplet is shown as a function of the temperature (T) for a system with 70 atoms a size L = 1000|a| and δg/g = 0.15. The green line corresponds with the number of atoms in the biggest droplet of the most contributing conﬁguration (the conﬁguration with the highest Zconf). The other lines correspond with the expectation value of the amount of droplets that have one, two or four atoms. The vertical dotted line corresponds with the approximation for the fragmentation temperature derived in Eq. (58). The vertical dashed line corresponds with the more complex approximation described in section 4.2.

Fig. 6 In Fig. a-c, the average fragmentation ⟨v⟩is given as a function of the temperature T for a 1D system. In Fig. a, ⟨v⟩is plotted for diﬀerent δg/g where L = 5000|a| and N = 70. In Fig. b this is plotted for diﬀerent number of particles (N) where L = 5000|a| and δg/g = 0.15. In Fig. c it is plotted for diﬀerent system sizes (L) where N = 70 and δg/g = 0.15. In Fig. d, the speciﬁc heat (CV ) is given as a function of the temperature for diﬀerent system sizes, where N = 70 and δg/g = 0.15. In all the subﬁgures the curves correspond to the calculations with the full conﬁgurations. The vertical colored lines correspond with the approximation in Eq. (58). Fig. 7 In Fig. a, the fragmentation temperature (Tf ) in the thermodynamic limit (where N is ﬁxed to 2048 atoms) for a 1D droplet as a function of δg/g and density (ρ = N/L) in units of the scattering length. The white line corresponds to the critical temperature of the droplets [24]. In Fig. b, Tf is given as a function of δg/g, for diﬀerent system sizes with the same density of atoms (ρ = 0.0512 |a|). Tf is in both ﬁgures calculated using the approximation in Eq. (58).
